title: Delocalization transition for light in two dimensions
authors: Sébastien Lucas, Christian Miniatura, Sergey E. Skipetrov
abstract: Common belief, confirmed by existing experiments, is that arbitrarily weak disorder should lead to spatial localization of eigenmodes of scalar wave equations when wave propagation is two-dimensional (2D). We predict that contrary to this belief, a localization-delocalization transition can take place for light scattered by two-level atoms placed at random positions in the middle plane of a parallel-plate 2D waveguide fed by its fundamental transverse-magnetic (TM) mode (electric field polarized perpendicular to the waveguide and atomic planes). This transition, driven by near-field dipole-dipole interactions between atoms, occurs upon increasing the areal number density of atoms beyond some critical value. A finite-size scaling analysis of the transition yields an estimate of its critical exponent $ν$ = 1.4 $\pm$ 0.2.
summary: This paper demonstrates that light in a 2D waveguide can undergo a localization-delocalization transition. While standard 2D scaling theory predicts that any disorder causes Anderson localization, the authors show that increasing the density of atoms triggers a transition to an extended regime. This transition is driven by strong near-field dipole-dipole interactions. The study provides a critical exponent for this transition and distinguishes between localized modes and subradiant atom pairs.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether a localization-delocalization transition can occur in a 2D system of light-scattering atoms, challenging the established belief that any amount of disorder in 2D leads to Anderson localization.
main_result: The authors predict a transition from Anderson localization to a delocalized regime driven by near-field dipole-dipole interactions as the atomic density increases, with an estimated critical exponent of nu = 1.4 +/- 0.2.
method: The study uses numerical analysis of the eigenvalues and quasi-normal modes of an effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian, combined with finite-size scaling and multifractal analysis.
model_or_system: A 2D planar waveguide formed by two parallel reflecting plates containing an ensemble of N identical two-level atoms randomly distributed in the middle plane, interacting via electromagnetic Green's functions.
key_observables: Decay rates (Gamma), Thouless conductance (g), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and multifractal dimensions (Dq).
important_parameters: Atomic areal number density (rho/k0^2), waveguide width (d), and frequency detuning.
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using an effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian; the scaling theory assumes single-parameter scaling holds near the critical line.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the waveguide geometry; Figure 2 compares decay rates in the waveguide vs 2D models to show the transition; Figure 3 and others show IPR and conductance scaling behavior.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of 2D localization, describes the waveguide model and Green's function, presents numerical results for decay rates and conductance, performs finite-size scaling to find the critical exponent, and discusses the fractal nature of the modes.
why_interesting: It challenges a fundamental tenet of 2D wave physics (that all disorder leads to localization) by showing how many-body-like dipole interactions can drive a phase transition to a delocalized state.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. N two-level atoms (red dots) are randomly dis- tributed in the z = 0 plane in the middle of a planar waveg- uide formed by two perfectly reflecting parallel mirrors (gray plates) separated by a distance d < π/k0. Such a planar waveguide only admits propagating TM0 modes whose elec- tric field, perpendicular to the mirrors, polarizes the atomic dipoles along z axis (black arrows) and is subsequently mul- tiply scattered by the atoms.

FIG. 2. Ensemble average of the decay rate of the longest- lived quasi-normal mode, as a function of the atomic density for different system sizes, for modes of our planar WG system (open diamonds) and for truly 2D modes (dots). The black dashed line corresponds to the decay rate of the most subra- diant mode localized on a pair of atoms [30].

FIG. 3. Mean-log inverse participation ratio ⟨ln IPR(2)⟩(a–c) and fractal dimension D2 (d–f) as functions of frequency for different densities ρ/k2 0 = 0.1 (top), ρ/k2 0 = 1 (middle), ρ/k2 0 = 10 (bottom) and different atom numbers N, for modes of both WG (open diamonds) and 2D (dots) models.

FIG. 4. (a) Scaling function β = d⟨ln g⟩/d ln k0R for WG model (1) versus frequency ω and atomic number density ρ. β is numerically computed from the dependence of ⟨ln g⟩on the atomic sample size R by a finite-difference approximation for N = 4000 and 10000. The dashed black line indicates β = 0. The large values β > 2 observed in some regions of the parameter plane are due to finite-size effects [30]. The hatched high-density domain at ω −ω0 > 0 corresponds to β ≃1 while the hatched high-density domain at ω −ω0 < 0 corresponds to β ≃2. (b) ⟨ln g⟩obtained from the data for (ω −ω0)/Γ0 ∈[0, 3], N = 4000, 8000, 10000, 12000 and ρ/k2 0 ∈[1, 3] as a function of R/ξ (symbols), and the best fit to...
